paragraphs.body: The Odd Object, LARGE subject, KNOWING AUTHOR Project I was sat on the couch, completely reclined with my feet dangling from the edge; my father to my right, vying in our chess match. Although physically in my comfort zone, it was always a nerve-wracking situation while facing my father in chess. His stature was extremely demanding, which branches in very well to our game. I always felt a sense of anxiety when playing him. But why though? Was it because I’ve never beaten him? Probably. Was it from a lack of confidence and motivation? Or was it all of the speculation of his childhood expertise? Although I didn’t know him at the time, I’ve heard hundreds of stories about his chess ability from his childhood. As a baseball fan, he used to call it his favorite “American past-time”. Unfortunately for me, this game was no different. Blunder after blunder sought me to an endgame with a singular knight against a queen (down 6 pieces of material). It seemed like another vulnerable state with little to no chance of winning. Staring at that knight, I could see the grooves in it’s head, symbolizing the scars it's rendered through battle. I see its eyes staring into the direction of both of my fathers pieces. Out of nowhere, the knight gave me an immense portion of motivation, something I’ve never felt before.

annotations.text: But why though? Was it because I’ve never beaten him? Probably. Was it from a lack of confidence and motivation? Or was it all of the speculation of his childhood expertise?

paragraphs.body: Although in this instance my enemy, my dad had always positioned himself to influence us in a way that his father couldn’t. My grandfather, who I never got to meet, never set the best precedent for his children. Based on the stories I’ve heard about him, I simply don’t know if I was lucky or unlucky to never meet him. My father had always described him with such a negative connotation that I never even wished to see him. Being one of 4 children and the only boy, he was always the one to stand up to his fathers antics, whether it was lashing out on his mother for messing up family dinner or yelling at his younger sister for misbehaving at school. The stories he tells me give me goosebumps every time. I could never imagine my father treating anyone in that way. Living in a small ranch in New Hyde Park, New York, he did everything for himself because they were so poor. He shoveled snow in the winter, mowed lawns in the spring, delivered newspapers in the fall, and worked as a lifeguard over the summer. With so much time working, it was still very unclear if he had enough to spend on his priorities. With three sisters and a mother, he was motivated by the thought of giving back to them. But apart from the gift of giving, he needed to provide for himself. How would he pay for college? How could he afford getting meals everyday? I never would’ve been able to do it. That’s just the person he is.

annotations.text: my dad had always positioned himself to influence us in a way that his father couldn’t.

paragraphs.body: Even with all of these problems, my father completed high school and became a first generation college student and decided to attend New York Tech. Although successful in the city, there were still a plethora of problems still happening on Long Island. With family in mind, he transferred to St. John’s University to stay closer to home and commute for the benefit of his siblings and mother. He was always motivated by the thought of his family's success. After 3 long years at St. John's motivations eventually swayed from protecting his three sisters and mother, to protecting America as a whole, and eventually enlisted in the military. From basic training to active duty, motivation remained his compass. Early on, he was pushed to his physical and mental limits. There were days when quitting seemed like the easiest option, and going back home to his poverty stricken family was bound to happen, but he stayed focused. He reminded himself why he joined. He sought to prove to himself that he could overcome any challenge. That mindset carried him through grueling field missions, long nights in unfamiliar places, and the pressure of keeping his team connected and safe as a communications specialist.

annotations.text: After 3 long years at St. John's motivations eventually swayed from protecting his three sisters and mother, to protecting America as a whole, and eventually enlisted in the military.

paragraphs.body: Soon, he was promoted to logistics officer, not only implementing what he learned through his four years in college, but additionally participating in combat to protect the lives of his fellow soldiers. Being logistics officer really motivated him to pursue a career in accounting after he serves in the military. But where does he start? He had barely any connections from college. He didn’t have much money to invest in himself. He didn’t even know where he was gonna live. He often described the military as doing what he loved best with the people he loved the best. Additionally, he would write down, “Motivation is when your dreams put work clothes on”, in his scrapbook everyday to read back on when he finally reached his point of success. I could imagine his near perfect handwriting going left to right as he wrote it. I can feel the deep breaths he took after reading it. I can taste the putrid air that he had to breathe in everyday in order to motivate his dreams.

paragraphs.body: Now, as I sit side by side with him on the couch, I look at my screen and see my knight. It means so much more than just a chess piece. I can envision the knight being my father losing to the queen, his father. Although the queen has more power, the knight has a long road ahead, full of hardship, motivation, and scrutiny in order to eventually win. The knight represented my fathers thoughts of protecting his family when his dad was crashing out over small things like the garden or a messy room. I see the knight as a trojan horse that my father rode into battle during war and his time in the army. Not only does the knight represent my dads motivation, but it also represents mine. I desire to live up to the expectations that my father has given to me. The knight can represent my challenges with anxiety and how motivation helps me overcome the common times of hardship. It displays the challenges of living six and a half hours away from home and to make my family proud even though I’m so far away. The knight is motivation. My forgotten sense of nervousness has suddenly transformed into an immaculate feeling of confidence. Although chess seems like such a stupid game, it taught me that any position of inferiority doesn’t result in losing. It’s important to see the bigger picture. This chess board represented my fathers everyday life. It was just my turn to experience it. Where did my journey begin? How do I motivate myself the way my father did? With so many questions in mind, it was time to take a page out of my dad’s book and stand up for myself when I was inferior. If this game taught me anything, it’s that a win would be the preface of the long novel about ’s hardships. And with one move in mind, checkmate.
